content.text: What Variables and Data Types Are

In Python, a variable is not a box that holds a value — it is a name bound to an object. When you write x = 42, Python creates an integer object with value 42 in memory and makes the name x point to it. The type lives on the object, not on the name. Reassigning x = 'hello' simply rebinds the name to a different object; the original integer is unchanged.

Data types define what kind of object a name points to at any given moment. Python's built-in scalar types — int, float, str, bool, bytes, and NoneType — are the primitive currency of all Python programs. Every AI pipeline, every tool call result, every configuration value starts life as one of these types before being composed into something larger.

Understanding the type system at this level — object identity, mutability, numeric towers, truthiness — prevents entire classes of bugs that surface only at runtime and are difficult to diagnose without this foundation.
Why It Matters

Python is dynamically typed: the interpreter infers the type of every expression at runtime, not at compile time. This makes the language fast to write but requires you to reason carefully about what type a variable holds at each point in your code. Silent type coercions — such as the fact that True + True == 2 — can produce incorrect results that pass superficial tests.

In the context of building AI systems, type discipline is especially important. LLM API responses arrive as strings or dictionaries. Numeric confidence scores are floats. Boolean flags control branching logic. None signals missing optional values. Getting any of these wrong — confusing a string '42' with the integer 42, or treating None as falsy without checking it explicitly — can corrupt an agent's decision-making without raising an exception.
Benefits of Understanding the Type System
Predict exactly how arithmetic, comparisons, and logical operators behave on your data.
Use isinstance() and type() to write defensive code that fails loudly instead of silently.
Annotate function signatures with type hints so editors and mypy catch type errors before runtime.
Understand why id(x) == id(y) does not imply x is meaningfully the same object as y unless both are int or str.
Write cleaner boolean logic by understanding Python's truthiness rules (what counts as falsy).
Avoid precision loss by choosing int over float when exact arithmetic is required.
Trade-offs
Dynamic typing speeds prototyping but pushes type errors to runtime — mitigate with type hints and mypy.
Python integers are arbitrary-precision; they are slower than machine integers for tight numeric loops — use NumPy arrays for bulk computation.
float uses IEEE-754 double precision: 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3 in Python, as in every language — use decimal.Decimal for exact financial arithmetic.
Strings are immutable; building a string with += in a loop is O(n^2) — use ''.join() for concatenation of many pieces.
NoneType has exactly one value; comparing with == None works but is == None is the idiomatic check — it catches subclasses that override __eq__.
How It Works
The Object Model

Every Python value is an object with three attributes: its identity (id()), its type (type()), and its value. The name you use to refer to the object is separate from the object itself. Multiple names can point to the same object (aliasing), and the same name can point to different objects over time (rebinding).

Python uses reference counting with a cyclic garbage collector to manage memory. When no name refers to an object, its memory is reclaimed. You never allocate or free memory manually, but understanding the object model helps you predict when objects are shared and when they are independent copies.
Integer Internals

Python caches small integers in the range [-5, 256]. Any two names bound to the integer 42 will share the same object; any two names bound to 1000 will not. This is why is works for small integers but not large ones — a fact that trips up many beginners who use is instead of == for integer comparison.
Float and Decimal

Python float is a 64-bit IEEE-754 double, giving roughly 15–17 significant decimal digits. The fraction 1/10 cannot be represented exactly in binary, so 0.1 + 0.2 evaluates to 0.30000000000000004. When exact decimal arithmetic matters — prices, percentages, measurements — use the decimal.Decimal type with an explicit precision context.
String Immutability

str objects are immutable sequences of Unicode code points. Once created, a string's characters cannot be changed in place. Operations such as upper(), replace(), and strip() always return new string objects; they never modify the original. This immutability makes strings safe to use as dictionary keys and allows Python to intern short strings for efficiency.
Boolean as Integer Subclass

bool is a subclass of int. True has the integer value 1 and False has the value 0. This means True + True == 2 is valid Python, and sum([True, False, True, True]) == 3. While occasionally useful for counting flags, it can be surprising and is best avoided when the intent is boolean logic rather than arithmetic.
None and NoneType

None is a singleton object of type NoneType. It is Python's null value, used to signal the absence of a meaningful result, an unset optional parameter, or a missing dictionary value. The idiomatic test is `if x is None` rather than `if x == None`, because is tests object identity (always correct for None) while == can be overridden by __eq__.
Architecture: Python Type System

attrs.alt: Python type system diagram: object hierarchy, dynamic typing vs type hints, variable-to-object binding model

content.text: When to Use Each Type
Type
Use when
Avoid when
int
Counting, indexing, exact whole-number arithmetic, bit flags
Floating-point math is needed
float
Scientific computation, ratios, probability scores
Exact decimal precision is required
decimal.Decimal
Monetary values, audit-grade arithmetic
Speed-critical numeric loops
str
Text, identifiers, serialized data, prompt content
Binary data (use bytes instead)
bool
Condition flags, truth tests
Arithmetic — prefer explicit int conversion
bytes
Binary file I/O, network protocols, encoded text
Text manipulation (decode to str first)
None
Sentinel for 'no value', optional return values
Default mutable arguments (use sentinel objects)
How to Implement
Declaring and Rebinding Variables

# Variables are created by assignment — no declaration keyword
x = 10          # x points to int object 10
y = 3.14        # y points to float object 3.14
name = "Claude" # name points to str object
active = True   # active points to bool True
result = None   # result points to NoneType None

# Rebinding: x now points to a str object; the int 10 is unchanged
x = "now a string"

# Multiple assignment (all names point to the same object)
a = b = c = 0

# Tuple unpacking — idiomatic Python
first, second, *rest = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
# first=1, second=2, rest=[3, 4, 5]
Inspecting Types

x = 42
print(type(x))           # <class 'int'>
print(isinstance(x, int))  # True
print(isinstance(x, bool)) # False  (42 is int but not bool)
print(id(x))             # e.g. 140234567890 (memory address)

y = True
print(type(y))            # <class 'bool'>
print(isinstance(y, int)) # True  (bool IS-A int)
print(int(y))             # 1

# Checking for None safely
value = None
if value is None:
    print("no value set")
Numeric Types and Arithmetic Precision

# Integer arithmetic is always exact
big = 2 ** 128    # Python int handles arbitrary size
print(big)        # 340282366920938463463374607431768211456

# Float precision
print(0.1 + 0.2)              # 0.30000000000000004
print(0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3)       # False

# Use decimal for exact arithmetic
from decimal import Decimal, getcontext
getcontext().prec = 28         # 28 significant digits

price = Decimal("19.99")
tax_rate = Decimal("0.085")
total = price * (1 + tax_rate)
print(total)                   # 21.6891... (exact)

# Integer division and modulo
print(17 // 5)   # 3   (floor division)
print(17 % 5)    # 2   (remainder)
print(17 / 5)    # 3.4 (true division, always float)
Type Conversion

# Explicit conversion (safe, raises ValueError on bad input)
n = int("42")          # str -> int
f = float("3.14")      # str -> float
s = str(100)           # int -> str
b = bool(0)            # 0 -> False; any non-zero -> True

# Truthiness rules — these all evaluate as False:
falsy_values = [False, 0, 0.0, "", [], {}, set(), None]
for v in falsy_values:
    assert not v, f"{v!r} should be falsy"

# These all evaluate as True:
truthy_values = [True, 1, -1, "x", [0], {"key": None}]
for v in truthy_values:
    assert v, f"{v!r} should be truthy"

# Dangerous implicit conversion to avoid:
# "3" + 3  ->  TypeError (Python does NOT auto-coerce here)
# In JS this would give "33"; Python requires explicit int("3") + 3
Type Hints

# PEP 484 type hints — checked by mypy/pyright, NOT enforced at runtime
from typing import Optional, Union

def parse_score(raw: str) -> float:
    return float(raw)

def get_label(score: Optional[float] = None) -> str:
    if score is None:
        return "unknown"
    return "positive" if score >= 0.5 else "negative"

# Python 3.10+ union syntax (no import needed)
def process(value: int | str | None) -> str:
    match value:
        case None:
            return "empty"
        case int(n):
            return f"number: {n}"
        case str(s):
            return f"text: {s}"

# Variable annotation (no initial value — no object created yet)
config_path: str   # mypy knows the type; name is unbound until assigned
Examples
Example 1: Agent Configuration Parser

"""
Parse and validate an agent configuration dictionary.
Demonstrates type checking, conversion, and None handling.
"""
from typing import Optional
from decimal import Decimal

def parse_agent_config(raw: dict) -> dict:
    """Convert raw config values to the correct Python types."""
    config = {}

    # str field — must be non-empty
    name = raw.get("name")
    if not isinstance(name, str) or not name.strip():
        raise ValueError("'name' must be a non-empty string")
    config["name"] = name.strip()

    # int field with default
    max_turns = raw.get("max_turns", 10)
    config["max_turns"] = int(max_turns)   # handles "10" or 10

    # float field in [0.0, 1.0]
    temp = raw.get("temperature", 0.7)
    config["temperature"] = float(temp)
    if not (0.0 <= config["temperature"] <= 1.0):
        raise ValueError("temperature must be between 0.0 and 1.0")

    # bool field — accept string "true"/"false" too
    raw_verbose = raw.get("verbose", False)
    if isinstance(raw_verbose, str):
        config["verbose"] = raw_verbose.lower() == "true"
    else:
        config["verbose"] = bool(raw_verbose)

    # Optional str field — None if absent
    system_prompt: Optional[str] = raw.get("system_prompt")
    config["system_prompt"] = system_prompt  # may be None

    return config

# --- Usage ---
raw_config = {
    "name": "research-agent",
    "max_turns": "20",       # string from JSON; will be cast to int
    "temperature": 0.3,
    "verbose": "true",       # string bool from env var
    # "system_prompt" absent — will be None
}

cfg = parse_agent_config(raw_config)
print(cfg)
# {
#   'name': 'research-agent', 'max_turns': 20,
#   'temperature': 0.3, 'verbose': True, 'system_prompt': None
# }
print(type(cfg["max_turns"]))   # <class 'int'>
print(type(cfg["temperature"])) # <class 'float'>
print(cfg["system_prompt"] is None)  # True
Example 2: Type-Aware Metric Accumulator

"""
Accumulate LLM run metrics, demonstrating int/float/bool/None
type discipline in a realistic agentic workflow context.
"""
from typing import Optional
import math

class RunMetrics:
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self.total_runs: int = 0
        self.successful_runs: int = 0
        self.total_tokens: int = 0
        self.latencies: list[float] = []
        self.last_error: Optional[str] = None

    def record(
        self,
        success: bool,
        tokens: int,
        latency_ms: float,
        error: Optional[str] = None,
    ) -> None:
        self.total_runs += 1            # int + int
        if success:
            self.successful_runs += 1
        self.total_tokens += tokens
        self.latencies.append(latency_ms)
        self.last_error = error         # None or str

    @property
    def success_rate(self) -> Optional[float]:
        if self.total_runs == 0:        # guard against division by zero
            return None
        return self.successful_runs / self.total_runs   # float

    @property
    def p50_latency(self) -> Optional[float]:
        if not self.latencies:
            return None
        sorted_l = sorted(self.latencies)
        mid = len(sorted_l) // 2
        return sorted_l[mid]

    def report(self) -> str:
        rate = self.success_rate
        rate_str = f"{rate:.1%}" if rate is not None else "N/A"
        p50 = self.p50_latency
        p50_str = f"{p50:.0f} ms" if p50 is not None else "N/A"
        return (
            f"Runs: {self.total_runs}  Success: {rate_str}  "
            f"Tokens: {self.total_tokens}  P50 latency: {p50_str}"
        )

metrics = RunMetrics()
metrics.record(success=True,  tokens=512,  latency_ms=320.5)
metrics.record(success=False, tokens=128,  latency_ms=85.0, error="rate_limited")
metrics.record(success=True,  tokens=1024, latency_ms=450.0)

print(metrics.report())
# Runs: 3  Success: 66.7%  Tokens: 1664  P50 latency: 321 ms
print(type(metrics.total_runs))     # <class 'int'>
print(type(metrics.success_rate))   # <class 'float'>
print(metrics.last_error)           # None  (last run succeeded)
Real-World Example: LLM Response Deserializer

LLM APIs return JSON that must be deserialized into typed Python objects. The response body is a string; token counts are integers; finish reason is a string enum; probabilities are floats; tool calls are optional dicts that may be None. A production deserializer must handle every type boundary explicitly.

"""
Deserialize an OpenAI-style chat completion response into typed fields.
Shows type introspection, Optional handling, and int/float/str discipline.
"""
from typing import Optional
import json

def deserialize_completion(response_json: str) -> dict:
    """
    Parse a raw API response string into a typed result dict.
    Returns a dict with guaranteed types for downstream consumers.
    """
    raw: dict = json.loads(response_json)   # str -> dict

    choices = raw.get("choices", [])
    if not choices:
        raise ValueError("Response contains no choices")

    choice = choices[0]
    message = choice.get("message", {})

    # str fields — may be None in some finish_reason values
    content: Optional[str] = message.get("content")
    finish_reason: str = choice.get("finish_reason", "unknown")

    # int fields from usage block
    usage = raw.get("usage", {})
    prompt_tokens: int = int(usage.get("prompt_tokens", 0))
    completion_tokens: int = int(usage.get("completion_tokens", 0))
    total_tokens: int = prompt_tokens + completion_tokens   # always exact

    # Optional nested dict — tool_calls may be None or absent
    tool_calls: Optional[list] = message.get("tool_calls")

    # bool derived from str comparison
    was_truncated: bool = finish_reason == "length"

    return {
        "content": content,              # Optional[str]
        "finish_reason": finish_reason,  # str
        "prompt_tokens": prompt_tokens,  # int
        "completion_tokens": completion_tokens,  # int
        "total_tokens": total_tokens,    # int
        "tool_calls": tool_calls,        # Optional[list]
        "was_truncated": was_truncated,  # bool
    }

sample = json.dumps({
    "choices": [{
        "message": {"role": "assistant", "content": "Hello, world!"},
        "finish_reason": "stop"
    }],
    "usage": {"prompt_tokens": 15, "completion_tokens": 5}
})

result = deserialize_completion(sample)
print(result["content"])          # Hello, world!
print(type(result["total_tokens"]))  # <class 'int'>
print(result["was_truncated"])    # False
print(result["tool_calls"] is None)  # True
Common Mistakes
Using == None Instead of is None
Always use `x is None` to check for None. Using `x == None` works for built-in types but can be fooled by objects that override __eq__. Linters flag this and mypy warns about it.
Using is for Integer Comparison

The expression `x is 42` may return True for small integers due to Python's integer cache, but it returns False for integers outside [-5, 256]. Never use is to compare values — use == for value equality and is only for identity (None, True, False, singletons).
Confusing str and int from JSON

JSON numbers become Python int or float; JSON strings become Python str. But environment variables, command-line arguments, and CSV fields are always strings. Forgetting to call int() on a numeric string before arithmetic gives a TypeError or silent string concatenation if the operand is also a string.
Mutating Through Aliases

When two names point to the same mutable object (list, dict), mutating through one name changes what the other sees. This is not a bug in Python — it is the expected behavior of the name-binding model — but it surprises newcomers who expect assignment to copy values.
Relying on Float Equality

Never compare floats with ==. Use math.isclose(a, b, rel_tol=1e-9) for approximate equality, or use round() when comparing to a known decimal value. The expression 0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3 evaluates to False.
Best Practices
Annotate every function parameter and return type with type hints; run mypy or pyright in CI.
Use `x is None` and `x is not None` — never `== None`.
Use `isinstance(x, int)` rather than `type(x) == int`; isinstance respects inheritance.
Prefer int over float when the value represents an exact count or index.
Use decimal.Decimal for monetary arithmetic; never float.
Avoid string concatenation in loops; use ''.join(parts) or an io.StringIO buffer.
Document the expected type of every function parameter in the docstring if the type hint alone is ambiguous.
Use explicit conversion (int(), float(), str()) rather than relying on implicit coercions.
Use bool() explicitly when converting to boolean; do not rely on truthiness of unexpected types.
Use the walrus operator (:=) in Python 3.8+ to bind and test in a single expression when it clarifies intent.
Performance and Scalability

Python's dynamic type system imposes a cost: every attribute lookup, every arithmetic operation, and every function call involves type dispatch at runtime. For most code this overhead is irrelevant. For tight numeric loops processing millions of values it is significant.
Use NumPy arrays (dtype=float64, int32, etc.) for bulk numeric computation — operations run in C, bypassing per-element Python overhead.
Use array.array for homogeneous sequences of machine scalars when memory layout matters but NumPy is too heavy a dependency.
Python integers beyond machine word size use arbitrary-precision arithmetic, which is slower; stay within 64-bit range for hot paths.
String interning: Python automatically interns identifiers and string literals; call sys.intern(s) to intern dynamically created strings used as dict keys.
Memory: a Python int object is 28 bytes on 64-bit Python (vs 8 bytes for a C long); a list of a million Python ints uses far more memory than a NumPy int64 array of the same count.
Tooling and Ecosystem
mypy — the reference static type checker; run in strict mode for production code.
pyright (Pylance in VS Code) — Microsoft's type checker, faster incremental checks.
beartype — runtime type checking decorator for enforcing type hints on function calls.
pydantic — data validation library built on type hints; used by FastAPI and many LLM frameworks for structured outputs.
typing module — Optional, Union, Literal, TypeVar, Protocol, and other annotation building blocks.
dataclasses — auto-generate __init__, __repr__, __eq__ from annotated class fields.
attrs — similar to dataclasses with more features; popular in CLI and agent frameworks.
decimal module — IEEE 854 arbitrary-precision decimal arithmetic.
Comparison with Alternatives
Language
Type System
Python Difference
Python
Dynamic, duck-typed; optional hints via PEP 484
Baseline
JavaScript
Dynamic, weakly typed; coerces across types silently
Python raises TypeError; JS silently produces NaN or '42'
TypeScript
Structurally typed, compile-time checks
Python hints not enforced at runtime without beartype/pydantic
Java
Nominally typed, statically compiled
Python vars need no declaration; types live on objects not variables
Go
Statically typed, compiled, no implicit conversion
Python is more flexible but less safe without linters
Rust
Affine types, ownership, no null
Python has GC and None; Rust has Option<T> and compile-time safety
Security and Reliability

Type errors in Python surface as exceptions, not as silent data corruption — provided you check types explicitly. In security-sensitive code, always validate the type and range of untrusted input before using it, even if it passed through an earlier deserialization step.
Never eval() or exec() untrusted strings — even type-correct strings can contain malicious code.
Validate numeric ranges (e.g., confidence in [0, 1]) not just types after deserialization.
Use secrets.token_hex() not random.randint() when generating security-sensitive bytes.
Be aware that large integers from untrusted JSON can consume arbitrary memory during arithmetic — set maximum length limits on parsed values.
Avoid trusting the `type` field in JSON messages from untrusted sources to determine processing logic.
Mental Model

Think of a Python variable as a sticky note label on a box (the object). You can move the label to a different box (rebind), you can stick multiple labels on the same box (aliasing), and you can read the box's contents through any label. The box knows its own type; the label does not. When you call type(x), you are asking the box — not the label — what kind of thing it is.

For immutable types (int, float, str, bool, tuple), the box's contents never change after creation. Operations that look like modifications — such as x += 1 for an integer — actually create a new box and move the label. For mutable types (list, dict, set), the box's contents can change in place, and all labels pointing to that box see the updated contents.
Key Takeaways
Variables are names bound to objects; type lives on the object, not the name.
Python is dynamically typed: you can rebind a name to any type at any time.
Use type hints (PEP 484) and a static checker (mypy/pyright) to get static-typing benefits without giving up flexibility.
bool is a subclass of int: True == 1, False == 0 — use this only when intentional.
None is a singleton: always test with `x is None`, never `x == None`.
int arithmetic is always exact; float arithmetic is IEEE-754 approximate.
Use decimal.Decimal for monetary or audit-grade arithmetic.
isinstance(x, T) is preferred over type(x) == T because it respects inheritance.
str is immutable Unicode; bytes is immutable binary; decode bytes to str before text manipulation.
Truthiness rules: 0, 0.0, '', [], {}, set(), None are all falsy; everything else is truthy.
